title: Generational Wealth

content.statement: Wealth must survive time, not just be created.
content.question: Is your financial plan about income, or about multi-generational wealth?
sop: List your current assets: owned property, investments, business equity, retirement accounts.
List what could erode them: debt, poor estate planning, lack of diversification, no succession plan.
Estimate annual Growth % and annual Loss % honestly for your net worth (see calculation, page 4).
Reinvest one income stream into an owned, appreciating asset instead of spending it.
Put a basic protection plan in place this quarter, will, beneficiaries, diversification, even a simple one.
questions: Is your financial plan about income, or about multi-generational wealth?
If you passed away tomorrow, would what you've built keep providing for the people after you, or would it stop with your paycheck?
What's one asset you own today that would still be producing value in 30 years without you managing it?
content.equation_example: Growth (% your net worth grows per year) minus Loss (% lost to debt, poor decisions, or depreciation per year) equals Longevity. Growth 8%/year minus Loss 3%/year equals 5%/year net. Rising net % means wealth compounds across time and generations; falling or negative % means it erodes faster than it's built, regardless of income.
content.closing_statement: Build wealth that survives you, not just supports you.

caption:
Principle Nº 181: Generational Wealth

Wealth must survive time, not just be created.

Ask yourself: Is your financial plan about income, or about multi-generational wealth?
